speaker: interviewer
text: --September 17, 1996. The interviewee is General Felix Sparks; interviewer, Toni [? Koch ?] Binstock. Interview is being conducted in Lakewood, Colorado, USA. And it's being conducted in English. And we were talking about your wartime experience, General. And I just wonder, did you ever hear any Nazi propaganda-- Axis Sally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes Yes. When we were going overseas from Norfolk, Virginia, to North Africa, we actually went to Oran, North Africa, which is in Algeria. We had a very large convoy, of course. Our division-- well, there are five ships just in our regiment, so-- that were needed to carry just our regiment. So we had-- with a naval escort, we probably had 50 vessels of one type or another, a large convoy. On the way over, we kept getting over the radio these messages from Axis Sally. But we thought they were very amusing. I recall she kept saying-- one day, she said, now, boys-- she-- the Germans identified us correctly. They, they-- we were a little surprised. We didn't-- but anyway, she came on and said, we know you boys from the 45th Infantry Division are sailing for North Africa. And she said, now, boys, I'm-- it's very sad-- and I'm very sad to do this. But I'm going to sing-- I'm going to play a song for you. And the song is 'The Last Round-Up.' And they had that American song, 'The Last Round-Up.' So she repeated that several times. And she had all kind of propaganda. We hardly ever got the chance to listen to radio, of course. But on the naval convoy, we didn't have anything to do. So we could listen to radio. When we were in combat, we didn't have any radio. I mean, we had radios, but not-- just for our own communication. But it took us a little over two weeks to from Norfolk to North Africa. And she entertained us the whole time. We thought it was hilarious. I guess she thought she was making some impression on us. But we thought it was funny. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then-- let's get back. You started to tell about the battle in southern France and, and how difficult that got. Would you continue about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes Yes. The South of France battle, it wasn't bad till we got some miles inland-- over a hundred miles inland and running into the Vosges Mountains. And the Vosges Mountains are very rugged, heavily timbered. And it was-- we were running into winter then. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The winter descended upon us. And there was a lot of snow and some-- and rain. And the weather was very cold. And it was very difficult to fight against the-- to attack the German fortified positions because they had time to dig in. So we just slogged it out. We took casualties every day. Maybe in my battalion, I would take a, a mountain-- take me all day to take a mountaintop The next day, I had to do the same thing on the next mountain. And it was very discouraging because we took very, very heavy casualties. And the weather was absolutely miserable. All the soldiers were miserable. And we were not getting many replacements in. So we were getting under strength. And sometimes, were in the line for-- fighting for over 60 days with no relief at all. We-- the American Army was getting stretched pretty thin then because of the advance to our left on the-- from the-- as a result of the Normandy invasion. And we were just running short on men. And then we had the war going on in the Pacific, as well. So the American Army was stretched pretty thin.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a thoroughly miserable and costly campaign that we put on to getting to the German border [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] from our initial landing. We lost-- in my regiment alone, we lost several hundred men. And I lost several hundred in my battalion.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in, in December of-- just the day before Christmas in 1944, my battalion pushed into Germany. And I wasn't the first. There were other American units that had already crossed the day before. But in my attack zone, I crossed the day before Christmas. And we went-- got into Germany then, and the next few days several miles. And we thought we were-- finally cracked the worst of it. But then came the Battle of the Bulge. And the Germans made a desperate attempt to break through the troops on them, which were way over to our left attacking into-- through Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that stalled everything. Then we had to pull some troops out of our army to send over to help the troops that were being attacked on our left. So we were stripped of troops, and-- to try to reinforce the Bulge. And that, that Bulge was finally stopped by, by Christmas, actually. It would-- or right after Christmas, it was pretty well stopped. But then the Germans made a second attack in our sector, which they called Operation North Wind. And we had a new division that had just come over from the States up in the Vosges Mountains. And the Germans attacked that division and pushed it back some 5 or 10 miles.

speaker: interviewer
text: So suddenly, I got an order-- I'm up in Germany now, and-- that I had to pull back because we were fairly exposed. Our division was up there by itself in Germany. So, well, the whole division was ordered back and immediately ordered to counter-attack the German penetration. So I got back one afternoon from Germany by trucks and hit the German penetration the next morning. That was in January of '45. And that was the most costly battle of the war for our regiment.

speaker: interviewee
text: We hit the German penetration head-on. And they had some good troops. They had some SS troops that they had just brought in from Norway, and-- well-equipped, well-trained. And we were at about half-strength from the exhaustion. Anyway, we fought there for a week in those mountains. And the Germans didn't gain another inch. But on the other hand, we paid for it dearly. Our regiment-- we lost five full rifle companies. I have nine companies in the regiment. We lost five full companies in that battle, along with elements of others.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we paid very dearly. And we were decimated. But the Germans did not gain any further. That was the worst battle of the war for us, by far. What did that do to you and your troops?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was-- it hurt badly because I lost three of my companies-- all three of my rifle companies. So out of what normally would be a thousand of men, I had less than 200 left. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were-- when we got the German drive stopped, then a new division came in and relieved us. And they didn't really have to do anything. just took over our positions. We were pulled back. And I got-- I was given about two weeks to formulate and train a new battalion to go back into combat. Well, that was a-- one of my darkest moments because [CRYING] I lost a lot of good men in the-- in that week battle. But in two weeks, new recruits started coming in. And I had to formulate-- get new officers, new company commanders, new sergeants. And it was a frantic job, trying to get organized a-- an infantry battalion again in a-- in about a two-week period. But we did it. And went back into the lines again. That's the way war works. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After that, then, we, we finally [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] fought through January, February. And the Germans were weakening all the time. And in March, then, the whole Allied Armies-- in March of '45, all the Allied Armies made a major push into Germany. Many were already-- over our left, they were already in the edge of Germany. In our area, we attacked in March. And we didn't stop. We had orders to keep moving, keep moving, keep moving. Don't let the Germans get reformed, reorganized.

speaker: interviewer
text: So even went-- in some cases, we went 24 hours a day, day and night. This was a-- not-- needless to say, it was tremendously exhausting. We used searchlights at night to see. And we made have several night attacks using searchlights to keep the Germans off balance. And it was very successful.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a bitter battle on the Siegfried Line, a heavily defended line that the Germans had built. But we, we broke-- in our sector, we broke it in one day. But again, we took some pretty heavy casualties. A couple of days later, we crossed the Rhine River. And by that time, the Germans were pretty badly disorganized. And in my sector, I crossed the Rhine-- and using assault boats furnished by the Corps of Engineers. And we had almost no opposition on the Rhine. So we crossed quite easily, drove on into-- deep into Germany, then, and kept driving. The, the only major battle we had after the Rhine River was at a city called Aschaffenburg, Germany, which was on the Main River, which, is a tributary of the Rhine. And there, we run into a fanatical group of Germans who were defending the city. And it took us a week to clear the city. And we took some pretty heavy casualties, but nothing like the Germans did. They took probably 5,000 casualties. We had-- in my battalion, I had about 200. And then my battalion and, and the other two battalions of the regiment cleared Aschaffenburg. But it took us a week. And, as I say, it was hard fighting. Village or town fighting is always difficult because it's very close. You see, you, you go around the corner, and you're you're getting shot at from a window or a cellar, basement. So the fighting is very close. It means that when you get hit, you get hit at very close range. And that's the case where most of the wounds are caused by rifles. You can't use artillery to amount to anything in a-- in a city. It's almost-- artillery is almost useless if you're actually fighting in there. So you have to do it by hand. That's a hand operation. That means grenades and rifles. And that was pretty costly. But there was a German garrison of about 5,000 people. And with less than that, we overcame it and-- in a week's time. From there on, then, we moved fairly rapidly towards Nuremberg. And the Germans then made another stand at Nuremberg. See, all this time, I am a lieutenant colonel. I'm a battalion commander at that time. In Nuremberg, we have some tough resistance for, like, one day. But it took us two or three days-- that's a large city. It took us two or three days to clear it. I had very few casualties, probably not more than 20 in my battalion in Nuremberg, very light casualties. But we captured several thousand Germans there. And then right outside of Nuremberg, then, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as we were-- the whole army was regrouping to make-- our objective was the city of Munich. That was the Seventh Army-- one of the Seventh Army objectives. Because that was the capital city of Bavaria. And we were-- Nuremberg, we were operating in Bavaria. And that was considered to be the transportation, supply, and command hub of Bavaria. And therefore, it was a critical objective as far as the Seventh Army. Our division was part of the Seventh United States Army. On our left was the Third United States Army. And our mission was to get to Munich as rapidly as possible before the Germans had a chance to consolidate defensive positions. Right outside of a-- as we left Nuremberg, the day-- the day before we departed, I was designated as a task force commander and given a battalion of tanks to-- with my infantry battalion, plus two artillery batteries and some engineers. And the tanks was-- a battalion of tanks, that's about 54 tanks. And my orders were to proceed as rapidly as possible towards Munich, to bypass any opposition if I got too much opposition, and that following forces would clean it up. But I was to barrel into Munich as rapidly as I could. Well, of course, with these tanks, I had all the tanks that the division had. I only had-- they only had one battalion. So I divided it up into two prongs. My executive officer took one prong. I took the other. And then we just smashed whatever we ran into with a-- we had such tremendous firepower. The Germans, there's only-- put up-- they would blow up bridges and defend places like that. But we had so much firepower that-- and we had the engineers coming up, bridging the rivers for us in fast, fast order. The American Corps of Engineers was remarkably organized for helping the combat troops. And they could put a bridge across a very large river in a fairly short time. And so wherever we ran into problems, we-- I'd get infantry across the river to protect the engineers. And they would come up and put their bridge across. And then I could get my tanks. Well, this continued in for several days. And we were cutting off, oh, like, 50 miles a day. That is, until my tanks ran out of gas, actually. And they would have to stop and refuel. Until-- and on the evening of April 28, 1945, I was about 30 miles or a little less from Munich. So I geared up to make the attack towards Munich, got my tanks refueled by midnight. And we were going to take off the daylight. I'm running into very little German opposition, just a few roadblocks. And that took us, oh, usually 20, 30 minutes to wipe out what was in our way. And then we were on our way again. Well, and that night, I got-- I got a word over, over our radio system that the Dachau Concentration Camp was in our zone of action the next day. And the-- this information was sent to all units, not just to me, all units in the Seventh Army. But it happened to be in my zone of attack. And the information was that if we went into Dachau, that it was a politically sensitive area. That's the way it came over the radio. Well, that doesn't mean a damn thing to me, really. I don't pay a whole lot of attention to it because my mission was to take Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't know what a concentration camp was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't know what a concentration camps was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd never heard of it. Neither had any American soldier. And so I-- well, we assumed from the word, of course, that they were concentration-- that they were prisoners there. But we had absolutely and utterly no information in the American Army about concentration camps. There might have been some at higher headquarters. But I doubt it because none of it was ever disseminated, at least to the troops.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't pay much attention to that, to tell you the truth. And the next morning, I looked at my map. And I saw where the concentration camp was, right outside of the city of Dachau. So I had my left force, commanded by my executive officer, pass to the left of the camp, attacking towards Munich, and my right force, that I was commanding, attacking to the right of the camp. I didn't want to get slowed down by any concentration camp because I didn't figure that was a military objective. I didn't see how I was going to harm me any.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mission was to take Munich. So I jumped off the next morning in that formation, in my two units. And the concentration camp was in-between my two units-- my right units and my left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was commanding the other unit? What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Major Kessler. He was my executive officer. And then I had the right unit. And we had the tanks divided between the two forces. Well, we had-- attacking in the outskirts of Munich-- Dachau is about 9 or 10 miles from Munich, the city of Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to Munich before Dachau, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were-- the city of Dachau was in my zone of action, which meant that I had to clear it. There was a river there, the Amper River-- I mean, the bridges that were going to be blown.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had-- the right company, I told them, you clear the city of Dachau. But if you run into any opposition that you can bypass, bypass it. And I'll have the reserve company come up and take care of it. I don't want to get slowed down. So we jumped off that morning in that configuration. Well, the Germans weren't putting up a great deal of resistance. I lost a few men. I had a few men killed and wounded that morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Went in-- somewhere around 11 o'clock that morning, I got an urgent radio message ordering me-- this is almost the exact wording of the message. You are to proceed immediately to capture the concentration camp at Dachau. Upon securing that camp, you are not to allow anyone in or out. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, I digested that. And I was quite angry because it meant then, I had to divide up my force. I couldn't pull the two attack units out. But I had a reserve company that I had following. I had two companies in attack, and then a reserve company was following.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that meant, then, I had to deploy my reserve company to take the concentration camp. And that is-- from a tactical viewpoint, is, is very poor practice. Now my battalion is split up. And I had no longer can-- it's no longer self-supporting, that is, no mutual support. Because here my reserve company has taken on another mission.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was very angry about it. But I don't have any choice. What-- I wanted to get on and say, well, send the reserve battalion in there, see. That's what they should have done. But there was a battalion in reserve following me. Well, anyway, since I was the closest to it, they-- somebody was antsy to get it right away. Well, I then got-- I immediately got ahold of the reserve company commander, which was Company I of the 157th and explained the mission to him. And the camp was a little-- oh, maybe a mile off to the left of where I Company was. And I said, I don't know why or what we're, we're going to run into there. But I'll go with you. And I'll give you an extra platoon of machine guns.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took a machine of-- a platoon of machine guns from I Company-- or from M Company, which is a heavy weapons company, and attached it to I Company, so they'd have extra firepower. I did not take any tanks because the tanks were already committed. I didn't think I'd need tanks anyway. So somewhere around afternoon, around 1 o'clock, we approached the camp [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then could, could see the walls. You couldn't see anything inside because it just looked like an ordinary German military post to us from the outside. There was a masonry fence maybe 8 feet high or something like that around where we entered. And we saw the entrance to the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: But from the outside, it was just like a regular, German military post. Well, then I looked down the street and I saw the main gate, I decided I would not go in that way because if they were going to defend it, that would be the most likely place to defend. So I had the company deploy off to the left of the gate. And, at that time, then we ran into a railway [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] train that had 39 cars in it, various types of boxcars. Some of-- some of them were old passenger cars. Some were open, gondola type, like coal cars, or [? over ?] cars. Then there was a spur line leading from Dachau, the city of Dachau, into the concentration camp. And the-- on that train, there were-- each car was filled with human bodies. And they were all dead, as far as we could determine. Anyway, we searched through the-- these-- about 40 cars. And as-- all we found was dead, dead people in there. Obviously civilians-- that they had on-- well, they had-- some of them had on striped uniforms. They had on miscellaneous clothing. Some had had enough strength to crawl out and have-- fell on the ground near the railway car. And there were about 2,000 dead in there. Well, my men-- there was a lot of-- much, much said. But I could see the men were getting very angry. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So then I gave the order, well, let's, let's go into the camp. Did you know what was-- who these civilians were that were dead?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't have any idea where they came from, or what they were doing there. As all we knew is we found a railway car with-- as I recall, there were 39 cars. And each was filled with dead bodies. And they were horribly emaciated. They were skin and bones. And evidently, they'd been shut up in those cars, and whether they were being taken out or brought in, we did not know. We later found out that they had been brought in from Buchenwald. And probably they were on the road at least two weeks because we were bombing the tracks. And they got no food or water. And they just died terrible deaths. And we saw immediately that they have been starved to death and been shut up. And they just all died from lack of food and water.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was their smell? Was there an odor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, some of them had been dead for several days. And there was quite an odor. And some of us-- and just a few had had-- I don't know exactly what happened. But when the Germans deserted that train, some of the car-- they opened some of the doors to the boxcars. I don't-- I, I don't know why. I suppose they checked it out to see if anybody was still alive, I think. But they had deserted. We find out that most of the German-- the SS troops had deserted Dachau about two to three days before we arrived. They knew we were coming, and they wanted to get out of there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was our first evidence. Then, then we begun to have a feeling of what we were going to find inside the camp. Were there men and women and children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There weren't any-- there were some women, dead women in the train-- in the train cars. No children-- I never saw a child at any time at Dachau. There were a small number of women.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we'll stop the tape now and then continue the next one.
